lesson.title: Earthing Systems and ADS Components
lesson.subject: C&G 2365
lesson.tonePrompt: Teach like a strong electrical tutor: practical, direct, technically precise, concise, and natural.

steps.title: Intro
steps.objective: Orient the learner to Earthing Systems and ADS Components.
anchorFacts: This lesson focuses on Earthing Systems and ADS Components.
taskConstraints: Introduce the lesson naturally, but do not start the first teaching chunk yet.

steps: Explanation A
Explanation B
TT Earthing Systems
TN-S and TN-C-S Systems
CPC and Earthing Conductor

steps.title: Explanation A: The Role of ADS
steps.objective: Explanation A
keyIdeas: ADS stands for Automatic Disconnection of Supply. It is the primary safety measure used to prevent electric shock in an installation.
When a fault occurs—such as a live wire touching a metal casing—the ADS system must detect the fault and 'auto-kill' the power almost instantly.
ADS is not just a single device; it is a combination of the earthing system and a protective device, such as a fuse or circuit breaker.

The earthing system provides a low-resistance path for the fault current to flow. This high current then causes the protective device to trip or blow, disconnecting the supply before a person can be harmed by touching the live metalwork.
Explanation A
anchorFacts: ADS stands for Automatic Disconnection of Supply. It is the primary safety measure used to prevent electric shock in an installation.
When a fault occurs—such as a live wire touching a metal casing—the ADS system must detect the fault and 'auto-kill' the power almost instantly.
ADS is not just a single device; it is a combination of the earthing system and a protective device, such as a fuse or circuit breaker.

The earthing system provides a low-resistance path for the fault current to flow. This high current then causes the protective device to trip or blow, disconnecting the supply before a person can be harmed by touching the live metalwork.
ADS is the correct expansion for this abbreviation.
misconceptionsToWatch: Think about how a fuse knows when to blow; it needs a path for the fault current to travel through.
steps.questionIntent: Explanation A

basicQuestions.questionText: What does the abbreviation ADS stand for?
What is the primary safety purpose of the ADS measure?
Besides the earthing system, what other component is required to disconnect the power during a fault?

steps.deeperQuestionText: What is the main way the earthing system enable a protective device to operate during a fault?
steps.deeperQuestionIntent: Explanation A
deeperAnswerGuidance: Provides a low-resistance path, Allows high fault current to flow, Triggers the fuse or circuit breaker, and Disconnects the supply quickly
steps.hint: Think about how a fuse knows when to blow; it needs a path for the fault current to travel through.

steps.title: Explanation B: The Main Earthing Terminal and Bonding
steps.objective: Explanation B
keyIdeas: Every installation has a central safety hub called the Main Earthing Terminal, or MET. The MET is the master junction point where all protective conductors meet.
One of the key connections to the MET is the Main Protective Bonding Conductor. This conductor is used to connect 'extraneous-conductive-parts' to the earthing system.
Extraneous-conductive-parts are metal items that are not part of the electrical system but could introduce a voltage from outside, such as metallic gas, oil, or water pipes.

By linking these pipes to the MET, we ensure that all accessible metal parts stay at the same voltage (potential). This prevents a dangerous voltage difference that could cause a shock if you touched a pipe and a faulty appliance at the same time.
Explanation B

One of the key connections to the MET is the Main Protective Bonding Conductor. This conductor is used to connect 'extraneous-conductive-parts' to the earthing system.
Extraneous-conductive-parts are metal items that are not part of the electrical system but could introduce a voltage from outside, such as metallic gas, oil, or water pipes.

By linking these pipes to the MET, we ensure that all accessible metal parts stay at the same voltage (potential). This prevents a dangerous voltage difference that could cause a shock if you touched a pipe and a faulty appliance at the same time.
Main Protective Bonding Conductor is the name of the central hub where all protective conductors meet.
misconceptionsToWatch: The MET is the 'meeting point' for all safety wires in the building.
steps.questionIntent: Explanation B

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the name of the central hub where all protective conductors meet?
Which conductor is used to connect extraneous-conductive-parts to the MET?
What is a technical example of an extraneous-conductive-part?

steps.deeperQuestionText: What is the main reason metallic water pipes be connected to the MET even though they are not part of the electrical system?
steps.deeperQuestionIntent: Explanation B
deeperAnswerGuidance: Ensures all metal parts are at the same voltage, Maintains the same potential, Prevents a dangerous voltage difference, and Stops shock between pipes and appliances
steps.hint: The MET is the 'meeting point' for all safety wires in the building.

steps.title: TT Earthing Systems
steps.objective: Identify TT systems and explain the role of the local earth electrode.
keyIdeas: In a TT system, the letters stand for 'Terre-Terre' (French for Earth-Earth).
The first T indicates that the source (the supplier's transformer) is connected to Earth. The second T indicates that the consumer's installation is connected to Earth via a local earth electrode.
An earth electrode is a physical metal rod, plate, or mesh driven into the soil at the property. In a TT arrangement, the electricity supplier does not provide an earth terminal to the consumer.

Because soil has a higher resistance than a metal wire, TT systems usually cannot rely on a standard fuse alone for protection. They typically require an RCD (Residual Current Device) to ensure the supply is disconnected quickly during a fault.
Identify TT systems and explain the role of the local earth electrode.
anchorFacts: In a TT system, the letters stand for 'Terre-Terre' (French for Earth-Earth).
The first T indicates that the source (the supplier's transformer) is connected to Earth. The second T indicates that the consumer's installation is connected to Earth via a local earth electrode.
An earth electrode is a physical metal rod, plate, or mesh driven into the soil at the property. In a TT arrangement, the electricity supplier does not provide an earth terminal to the consumer.

Because soil has a higher resistance than a metal wire, TT systems usually cannot rely on a standard fuse alone for protection. They typically require an RCD (Residual Current Device) to ensure the supply is disconnected quickly during a fault.
Terra is the correct expansion for this abbreviation.
TT is the correct technical answer for this question.
misconceptionsToWatch: In a TT system, the 'T' at the end means the consumer must provide their own connection to the ground.
steps.questionIntent: Identify TT systems and explain the role of the local earth electrode.

basicQuestions.questionText: Which earthing system uses a local earth electrode at the installation?
Which protective device is usually required on a TT system because the earth path is high resistance?
Who provides the earth electrode in a TT system?

steps.deeperQuestionText: What is the main reason a TT system usually need an RCD instead of relying only on a fuse or MCB?
steps.deeperQuestionIntent: Identify TT systems and explain the role of the local earth electrode.
deeperAnswerGuidance: Fault current is too low, Earth path resistance is high, and An RCD can trip on small imbalance current
steps.hint: TT relies on the local earth, so the fault path is usually higher resistance.

steps.title: TN-S and TN-C-S Systems
steps.objective: Distinguish between TN-S and TN-C-S arrangements using the code letters and supply path.
keyIdeas: TN systems use the supplier's cable to provide the earth path back to the transformer. The T stands for Earth, and the N stands for Neutral.

In a TN-S system, the S stands for 'Separate.' This means the Neutral and Earth conductors are separate wires all the way from the transformer to the consumer. Usually, the lead or steel sheath of the supply cable acts as this separate earth path.

In a TN-C-S system, the C-S stands for 'Combined-Separate.' The Neutral and Earth are 'Combined' into a single conductor (called a PEN conductor) in the street, but they are 'Separated' at the consumer's intake (the cut-out).
TN-C-S is the most common modern system and is often referred to as PME, which stands for Protective Multiple Earthing.
Distinguish between TN-S and TN-C-S arrangements using the code letters and supply path.
anchorFacts: TN systems use the supplier's cable to provide the earth path back to the transformer. The T stands for Earth, and the N stands for Neutral.

In a TN-S system, the S stands for 'Separate.' This means the Neutral and Earth conductors are separate wires all the way from the transformer to the consumer. Usually, the lead or steel sheath of the supply cable acts as this separate earth path.

In a TN-C-S system, the C-S stands for 'Combined-Separate.' The Neutral and Earth are 'Combined' into a single conductor (called a PEN conductor) in the street, but they are 'Separated' at the consumer's intake (the cut-out).
TN-C-S is the most common modern system and is often referred to as PME, which stands for Protective Multiple Earthing.
Separate is the correct expansion for this abbreviation.
Combined is the correct expansion for this abbreviation.
misconceptionsToWatch: Look at the last letters: S means Separate wires, while C-S means they start Combined and then become Separate.
steps.questionIntent: Distinguish between TN-S and TN-C-S arrangements using the code letters and supply path.

basicQuestions.questionText: What does the letter S stand for in TN-S?
What does the letter C stand for in TN-C-S?
What is the technical name for the conductor that combines protective earth and neutral on the supply side?

steps.deeperQuestionText: Why is TN-C-S also described as a system where neutral and earth are combined first and then separated?
steps.deeperQuestionIntent: Distinguish between TN-S and TN-C-S arrangements using the code letters and supply path.
deeperAnswerGuidance: Because the supply uses a PEN conductor first, Because earth and neutral are combined on part of the supply, and They are separated at the service position
steps.hint: S = Separate, C = Combined, PEN = Protective Earth and Neutral.

steps.title: CPC and Earthing Conductor
steps.objective: Explain the difference between a CPC and an Earthing Conductor in ADS.
keyIdeas: We use specific names for earth wires based on their location in the system. A Circuit Protective Conductor, or CPC, is the earth wire that protects an individual circuit.

The CPC runs alongside the live and neutral wires in every cable (like the bare wire in Twin and Earth) to ensure that if a fault occurs in a specific light or socket, the current has a path back to the MET.
The Earthing Conductor is different; it is the single, main cable that connects the Main Earthing Terminal (MET) to the means of earthing (the supplier's terminal or a local rod).
To remember the difference: the CPC is for the 'branch' (the circuit), while the Earthing Conductor is the 'trunk' that connects the whole building to the Earth source.
Explain the difference between a CPC and an Earthing Conductor in ADS.
anchorFacts: We use specific names for earth wires based on their location in the system. A Circuit Protective Conductor, or CPC, is the earth wire that protects an individual circuit.

The CPC runs alongside the live and neutral wires in every cable (like the bare wire in Twin and Earth) to ensure that if a fault occurs in a specific light or socket, the current has a path back to the MET.
The Earthing Conductor is different; it is the single, main cable that connects the Main Earthing Terminal (MET) to the means of earthing (the supplier's terminal or a local rod).
To remember the difference: the CPC is for the 'branch' (the circuit), while the Earthing Conductor is the 'trunk' that connects the whole building to the Earth source.
CPC is the correct expansion for this abbreviation.
Circuit Protective Conductor is the correct expansion for this abbreviation.
Earthing Conductor is the name of the conductor that connects the MET to the source of earth.
misconceptionsToWatch: The CPC is inside the cables going to your sockets; the Earthing Conductor is the main green-and-yellow wire at the service head.
steps.questionIntent: Explain the difference between a CPC and an Earthing Conductor in ADS.

basicQuestions.questionText: What does CPC stand for?
What is the name of the conductor that connects the MET to the source of earth?
Which conductor connects exposed conductive parts in a circuit back to the MET?

steps.deeperQuestionText: Why is it important not to confuse a CPC with an earthing conductor during testing and documentation?
steps.deeperQuestionIntent: Explain the difference between a CPC and an Earthing Conductor in ADS.
deeperAnswerGuidance: They have different roles, One protects circuits and one links the MET to earth, and Using the wrong term causes technical errors
steps.hint: CPC protects the circuit; the earthing conductor links the MET to the source of earth.

steps.title: Worked Example: Identifying Earthing Components
steps.objective: Correctly identify the earthing system type and the specific names of the conductors used in an ADS setup.
keyIdeas: Show one worked example.

Scenario: You are inspecting a modern domestic property. At the service head (the cut-out), you see that the supplier has provided an earth terminal that is linked directly to the neutral. A 16mm² green-and-yellow cable runs from this terminal to a central copper busbar.
Step 1: Identify the Earthing System. Because the neutral and earth are combined in the supplier's cable but separated at the intake point, this is a TN-C-S system (often called PME).

Step 2: Identify the Main Earthing Conductor. The 16mm² cable connecting the supplier's terminal to the copper busbar (the Main Earthing Terminal or MET) is the Earthing Conductor. It is the primary link to the earth source.

Step 3: Identify the Bonding. You see 10mm² cables running from the MET to the incoming gas and water services. These are the Main Protective Bonding Conductors, used to keep all metalwork at the same potential.

Step 4: Identify the Circuit Protection. Looking inside the consumer unit, you see the green-and-yellow cores of the twin-and-earth cables connected to the earth bar. These are the Circuit Protective Conductors (CPCs).

taskSkeleton.scenario: You are inspecting a modern domestic property. At the service head (the cut-out), you see that the supplier has provided an earth terminal that is linked directly to the neutral. A 16mm² green-and-yellow cable runs from this terminal to a central copper busbar.
steps: Identify the Earthing System. Because the neutral and earth are combined in the supplier's cable but separated at the intake point, this is a TN-C-S system (often called PME).

Identify the Main Earthing Conductor. The 16mm² cable connecting the supplier's terminal to the copper busbar (the Main Earthing Terminal or MET) is the Earthing Conductor. It is the primary link to the earth source.

Identify the Bonding. You see 10mm² cables running from the MET to the incoming gas and water services. These are the Main Protective Bonding Conductors, used to keep all metalwork at the same potential.

Identify the Circuit Protection. Looking inside the consumer unit, you see the green-and-yellow cores of the twin-and-earth cables connected to the earth bar. These are the Circuit Protective Conductors (CPCs).
taskSkeleton.takeaway: Precision matters. The 'Earthing Conductor' is the main link to the source, the 'CPC' is for the individual circuit, and 'Bonding' is for the pipes. They are not interchangeable terms.

steps.hint: Focus on where the wire starts and ends. If it goes to a pipe, it's bonding. If it goes to a socket, it's a CPC. If it goes to the supplier's terminal, it's the Earthing Conductor.

steps.title: Integrative Close: Why TT Uses an RCD
steps.objective: Explain why TT systems usually need an RCD.
keyIdeas: TT systems rely on soil for the return path, which has much higher resistance than a metal return path.
High earth path resistance limits fault current, so a fuse or MCB may not trip quickly enough.
An RCD is used because it trips on imbalance rather than needing a large fault current.
anchorFacts: High soil resistance means low fault current in a TT system.
An RCD can disconnect even when the fault current is too small for a fuse or MCB to operate quickly.
taskConstraints: Keep the response to one short explanation.
Require the learner to link high resistance to low fault current and then to RCD operation.
steps.questionIntent: Explain why TT systems usually require an RCD.

taskSkeleton.scenario: A TT installation depends on a local earth electrode and the earth path is relatively high resistance.
steps: Explain why this makes an RCD necessary.

steps.questionText: Using one short explanation, explain why TT systems usually require an RCD.
answerGuidance: fault current too low, high resistance earth path, and RCD trips on imbalance
steps.hint: The key issue is not the label TT by itself; it is the resistance of the earth return path.

steps.deeperQuestionText: What is the main reason a TT system usually needs an RCD instead of relying only on a fuse or MCB?
deeperAnswerGuidance: fault current too low
earth path resistance is high
RCD trips on imbalance
steps.hint: TT relies on the local earth, so the fault path is usually higher resistance.

steps.title: TN-S and TN-C-S Systems
steps.objective: Distinguish between TN-S and TN-C-S arrangements using the code letters and supply path.
keyIdeas: TN systems use the supplier's cable to provide the earth path back to the transformer. The T stands for Earth, and the N stands for Neutral.

In a TN-S system, the S stands for 'Separate.' This means the Neutral and Earth conductors are separate wires all the way from the transformer to the consumer. Usually, the lead or steel sheath of the supply cable acts as this separate earth path.

In a TN-C-S system, the C-S stands for 'Combined-Separate.' The Neutral and Earth are 'Combined' into a single conductor (called a PEN conductor) in the street, but they are 'Separated' at the consumer's intake (the cut-out).
TN-C-S is the most common modern system and is often referred to as PME, which stands for Protective Multiple Earthing.
anchorFacts: In TN-S, the Neutral and Earth are separate conductors throughout.
In TN-C-S, Neutral and Earth are combined in the supply (PEN) and separated at the intake.
TN-C-S is commonly known as PME.
Separate is the correct expansion for this abbreviation.
Combined is the correct expansion for this abbreviation.
misconceptionsToWatch: S means Separate; C means Combined. TN-C-S starts combined and ends separate.
steps.questionIntent: Differentiate between TN-S and TN-C-S systems.
steps.deeperQuestionIntent: Explain the physical arrangement of TN-C-S conductors.

basicQuestions.questionText: What does the letter S stand for in TN-S?
What does the letter C stand for in TN-C-S?
What is the technical name for the conductor that combines protective earth and neutral on the supply side?

steps.deeperQuestionText: Why is TN-C-S described as a system where neutral and earth are combined first and then separated?
deeperAnswerGuidance: supply uses a PEN conductor, earth and neutral combined on supply, and separated at service position
steps.hint: S = Separate, C = Combined, PEN = Protective Earth and Neutral.

steps.title: CPC and Earthing Conductor
steps.objective: Explain the difference between a CPC and an Earthing Conductor in ADS.
keyIdeas: We use specific names for earth wires based on their location in the system. A Circuit Protective Conductor, or CPC, is the earth wire that protects an individual circuit.

The CPC runs alongside the live and neutral wires in every cable (like the bare wire in Twin and Earth) to ensure that if a fault occurs in a specific light or socket, the current has a path back to the MET.
The Earthing Conductor is different; it is the single, main cable that connects the Main Earthing Terminal (MET) to the means of earthing (the supplier's terminal or a local rod).
To remember the difference: the CPC is for the 'branch' (the circuit), while the Earthing Conductor is the 'trunk' that connects the whole building to the Earth source.
anchorFacts: A CPC (Circuit Protective Conductor) protects an individual circuit.
The Earthing Conductor connects the MET to the source of earth.
The CPC and Earthing Conductor have distinct roles and locations.
Circuit Protective Conductor is the correct expansion for this abbreviation.
Earthing Conductor is the name of the conductor that connects the MET to the source of earth.
misconceptionsToWatch: The green-and-yellow wire in a socket cable is a CPC, not the Earthing Conductor.
steps.questionIntent: Distinguish between circuit-level and main-level earthing conductors.
steps.deeperQuestionIntent: Reinforce the functional difference between the two conductors.

basicQuestions.questionText: What does CPC stand for?
What is the name of the conductor that connects the MET to the source of earth?
Which conductor connects exposed conductive parts in a circuit back to the MET?

steps.deeperQuestionText: Why is it important not to confuse a CPC with an earthing conductor during testing and documentation?
deeperAnswerGuidance: different roles, CPC protects circuits, and Earthing conductor links MET to earth
steps.hint: CPC protects the circuit; the earthing conductor links the MET to the source of earth.

steps.title: Worked Example: Identifying Earthing Components
steps.objective: Correctly identify the earthing system type and the specific names of the conductors used in an ADS setup.
keyIdeas: Applying terminology to a real-world domestic intake scenario.
anchorFacts: A combined neutral/earth at the intake indicates TN-C-S.
The main link to the supplier terminal is the Earthing Conductor.
Wires to pipes are Bonding Conductors.

taskSkeleton.scenario: You are inspecting a modern domestic property. At the service head, you see the supplier earth terminal is linked directly to the neutral. A 16mm² green-and-yellow cable runs from this terminal to a central copper busbar (MET). 10mm² cables run from the MET to the gas and water pipes.
steps: Identify the Earthing System: Because neutral and earth are combined in the supply but separated at the intake, this is TN-C-S (PME).
Identify the Main Earthing Conductor: The 16mm² cable connecting the supplier's terminal to the MET is the Earthing Conductor.
Identify the Bonding: The 10mm² cables running to the gas and water services are Main Protective Bonding Conductors.
Identify the Circuit Protection: The green-and-yellow wires inside the circuit cables are the Circuit Protective Conductors (CPCs).
taskSkeleton.takeaway: Precision matters. The 'Earthing Conductor' is the main link to the source, the 'CPC' is for the individual circuit, and 'Bonding' is for the pipes.

steps.hint: Focus on where the wire starts and ends. If it goes to a pipe, it's bonding. If it goes to a socket, it's a CPC.

steps.title: Guided Practice: Identifying TT
steps.objective: Identify a TT system from the earthing arrangement described.
keyIdeas: TT systems rely on a local connection to the ground.
anchorFacts: A local earth rod or electrode identifies a TT system.
taskConstraints: Require one earthing-system answer only.
steps.questionIntent: Identify a TT earthing system from a local electrode arrangement.

taskSkeleton.scenario: A rural property has no earth terminal provided by the supplier. Instead, the electrician has driven a copper-clad steel rod into the ground to provide the earth connection.
steps: Identify the earthing system type from this arrangement.

steps.questionText: Which earthing system is described in this scenario?
steps.hint: A local earth electrode (rod) points to TT.

steps.title: Practice: Identifying TN-C-S
steps.objective: Identify TN-C-S from the combined-and-separated supply arrangement.
keyIdeas: TN-C-S uses a combined conductor in the supply network.
anchorFacts: A PEN conductor means protective earth and neutral are combined.
taskConstraints: Require one earthing-system answer only.
steps.questionIntent: Identify TN-C-S from a combined then separated supply arrangement.

taskSkeleton.scenario: In a suburban street, the supplier uses a single conductor for both Neutral and Earth functions. This conductor enters the house and is split into a separate Neutral and Earth at the consumer's cut-out.
steps: Identify the earthing system from this supply arrangement.

steps.questionText: Which earthing system is described in this scenario?
steps.hint: Combined first, then separated, points to TN-C-S.

issues.problem: The lesson ends at the Practice stage, but the final step points to a non-existent integrative step, and validation flags it as missing.
issues.whyItMatters: Integrative steps are crucial for beginners to synthesize separate concepts (like system types and conductor names) into a single coherent understanding of an installation.

issues.excerpt: trips the fuse
issues.problem: The answer guidance uses the phrase 'trips the fuse'.
issues.whyItMatters: Fuses 'blow' or 'melt'; circuit breakers 'trip'. Using imprecise terminology in marking guidance can reinforce incorrect technical language in beginners.
issues.suggestion: Change guidance to 'operates the protective device' or 'causes the fuse to blow / breaker to trip'.

issues.excerpt: Explanation A: The Role of ADS
issues.problem: The first two teaching steps use generic 'Explanation A/B' prefixes.
issues.whyItMatters: Beginners benefit from clear, topical signposting. Generic labels provide less cognitive orientation than the descriptive titles used in later steps.
issues.suggestion: Rename to 'The Role of ADS' and 'The Main Earthing Terminal (MET)' respectively.

issues.excerpt: What does the letter S stand for in TN-S?
issues.problem: Questions 1 and 2 in this step only test the recall of single letters rather than the concept of the arrangement.
issues.whyItMatters: While letter recall is a starting point, it doesn't confirm the learner understands the physical difference between separate and combined conductors.
issues.suggestion: Replace with a question asking where the Neutral and Earth are separated in a TN-S system compared to a TN-C-S system.

terms.simpleDefinition: Automatic Disconnection of Supply; a safety measure that cuts power quickly during a fault to prevent electric shock.
terms.misconception: Thinking ADS is just a single device like a fuse; it is actually the combination of earthing and protective devices working together.

terms.simpleDefinition: An earthing arrangement where the consumer provides their own connection to Earth using a local electrode.
terms.anchor: The 'Earth-to-Earth' system using a rod in the ground.
terms.misconception: Believing the electricity supplier provides the earth connection in a TT system.

terms.simpleDefinition: An earthing arrangement where the supplier provides a separate earth conductor (usually the cable sheath) back to the transformer.
terms.anchor: The 'Separate' system with a dedicated earth path from the source.
terms.misconception: Confusing the cable sheath earth with a combined neutral-earth conductor.

terms.simpleDefinition: An earthing arrangement where neutral and earth are combined in the supply cable but separated at the consumer's intake.
terms.anchor: The 'Combined' supply system, often called PME.
terms.misconception: Thinking the earth and neutral remain combined throughout the entire internal installation.

terms.simpleDefinition: Circuit Protective Conductor; the specific earth wire that protects an individual circuit.
terms.misconception: Using the term CPC to describe the main earthing conductor that connects to the supplier's terminal.

terms.simpleDefinition: The main conductor that connects the installation's Main Earthing Terminal (MET) to the means of earthing.
terms.anchor: The master link between the house and the Earth source.
terms.misconception: Confusing this with bonding conductors which connect to gas or water pipes.

terms.simpleDefinition: A metal rod, plate, or mesh driven into the ground to provide a physical connection to Earth.
terms.anchor: The physical spike in the soil.
terms.misconception: Thinking every house needs an electrode; they are primarily required for TT systems.

terms.simpleDefinition: A conductor used to connect extraneous-conductive-parts (like metal water pipes) to the Main Earthing Terminal.
terms.anchor: The safety link for incoming metal services.
terms.misconception: Thinking bonding conductors are meant to carry fault current from a circuit like a CPC does.

anchorPhrases: TT uses a local earth electrode.
TN-S has a separate earth path back to the source.
TN-C-S combines neutral and earth in the supply.
The CPC protects the individual circuit.
The Earthing Conductor connects the MET to the earth source.
misconceptionTargets: Terms in this lesson are distinct and must be used precisely.
The CPC and Earthing Conductor have different roles in the ADS path.
The supplier only provides the earth terminal in TN systems, not TT.

{
  "teachChecks": [
    {
      "key": "203-4a-teach-check-1",
      "title": "Explanation A: The Role of ADS",
      "objective": "Explanation A",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "ADS stands for Automatic Disconnection of Supply. It is the primary safety measure used to prevent electric shock in an installation.",
        "When a fault occurs—such as a live wire touching a metal casing—the ADS system must detect the fault and 'auto-kill' the power almost instantly.",
        "ADS is not just a single device; it is a combination of the earthing system and a protective device, such as a fuse or circuit breaker.",
        "The earthing system provides a low-resistance path for the fault current to flow. This high current then causes the protective device to trip or blow, disconnecting the supply before a person can be harmed by touching the live metalwork."
      ],
      "hint": "Think about how a fuse knows when to blow; it needs a path for the fault current to travel through."
    },
    {
      "key": "203-4a-teach-check-2",
      "title": "Explanation B: The Main Earthing Terminal and Bonding",
      "objective": "Explanation B",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "Every installation has a central safety hub called the Main Earthing Terminal, or MET. The MET is the master junction point where all protective conductors meet.",
        "One of the key connections to the MET is the Main Protective Bonding Conductor. This conductor is used to connect 'extraneous-conductive-parts' to the earthing system.",
        "Extraneous-conductive-parts are metal items that are not part of the electrical system but could introduce a voltage from outside, such as metallic gas, oil, or water pipes.",
        "By linking these pipes to the MET, we ensure that all accessible metal parts stay at the same voltage (potential). This prevents a dangerous voltage difference that could cause a shock if you touched a pipe and a faulty appliance at the same time."
      ],
      "hint": "The MET is the 'meeting point' for all safety wires in the building."
    },
    {
      "key": "203-4a-teach-check-3",
      "title": "TT Earthing Systems",
      "objective": "Identify TT systems and explain the role of the local earth electrode.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "In a TT system, the letters stand for 'Terre-Terre' (French for Earth-Earth).",
        "The first T indicates that the source (the supplier's transformer) is connected to Earth. The second T indicates that the consumer's installation is connected to Earth via a local earth electrode.",
        "An earth electrode is a physical metal rod, plate, or mesh driven into the soil at the property. In a TT arrangement, the electricity supplier does not provide an earth terminal to the consumer.",
        "Because soil has a higher resistance than a metal wire, TT systems usually cannot rely on a standard fuse alone for protection. They typically require an RCD (Residual Current Device) to ensure the supply is disconnected quickly during a fault."
      ],
      "hint": "In a TT system, the 'T' at the end means the consumer must provide their own connection to the ground."
    },
    {
      "key": "203-4a-teach-check-4",
      "title": "TN-S and TN-C-S Systems",
      "objective": "Distinguish between TN-S and TN-C-S arrangements using the code letters and supply path.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "TN systems use the supplier's cable to provide the earth path back to the transformer. The T stands for Earth, and the N stands for Neutral.",
        "In a TN-S system, the S stands for 'Separate.' This means the Neutral and Earth conductors are separate wires all the way from the transformer to the consumer. Usually, the lead or steel sheath of the supply cable acts as this separate earth path.",
        "In a TN-C-S system, the C-S stands for 'Combined-Separate.' The Neutral and Earth are 'Combined' into a single conductor (called a PEN conductor) in the street, but they are 'Separated' at the consumer's intake (the cut-out).",
        "TN-C-S is the most common modern system and is often referred to as PME, which stands for Protective Multiple Earthing."
      ],
      "hint": "Look at the last letters: S means Separate wires, while C-S means they start Combined and then become Separate."
    },
    {
      "key": "203-4a-teach-check-5",
      "title": "CPC and Earthing Conductor",
      "objective": "Explain the difference between a CPC and an Earthing Conductor in ADS.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "We use specific names for earth wires based on their location in the system. A Circuit Protective Conductor, or CPC, is the earth wire that protects an individual circuit.",
        "The CPC runs alongside the live and neutral wires in every cable (like the bare wire in Twin and Earth) to ensure that if a fault occurs in a specific light or socket, the current has a path back to the MET.",
        "The Earthing Conductor is different; it is the single, main cable that connects the Main Earthing Terminal (MET) to the means of earthing (the supplier's terminal or a local rod).",
        "To remember the difference: the CPC is for the 'branch' (the circuit), while the Earthing Conductor is the 'trunk' that connects the whole building to the Earth source."
      ],
      "hint": "The CPC is inside the cables going to your sockets; the Earthing Conductor is the main green-and-yellow wire at the service head."
    }
  ]
}

teachChecks.title: Explanation A: The Role of ADS
retrievalTextLines: ADS stands for Automatic Disconnection of Supply. It is the primary safety measure used to prevent electric shock in an installation.
When a fault occurs—such as a live wire touching a metal casing—the ADS system must detect the fault and 'auto-kill' the power almost instantly.
ADS is not just a single device; it is a combination of the earthing system and a protective device, such as a fuse or circuit breaker.

The earthing system provides a low-resistance path for the fault current to flow. This high current then causes the protective device to trip or blow, disconnecting the supply before a person can be harmed by touching the live metalwork.
teachChecks.hint: Think about how a fuse knows when to blow; it needs a path for the fault current to travel through.

teachChecks.title: Explanation B: The Main Earthing Terminal and Bonding
retrievalTextLines: Every installation has a central safety hub called the Main Earthing Terminal, or MET. The MET is the master junction point where all protective conductors meet.
One of the key connections to the MET is the Main Protective Bonding Conductor. This conductor is used to connect 'extraneous-conductive-parts' to the earthing system.
Extraneous-conductive-parts are metal items that are not part of the electrical system but could introduce a voltage from outside, such as metallic gas, oil, or water pipes.

By linking these pipes to the MET, we ensure that all accessible metal parts stay at the same voltage (potential). This prevents a dangerous voltage difference that could cause a shock if you touched a pipe and a faulty appliance at the same time.
teachChecks.hint: The MET is the 'meeting point' for all safety wires in the building.

teachChecks.title: TT Earthing Systems
teachChecks.objective: Identify TT systems and explain the role of the local earth electrode.
retrievalTextLines: In a TT system, the letters stand for 'Terre-Terre' (French for Earth-Earth).
The first T indicates that the source (the supplier's transformer) is connected to Earth. The second T indicates that the consumer's installation is connected to Earth via a local earth electrode.
An earth electrode is a physical metal rod, plate, or mesh driven into the soil at the property. In a TT arrangement, the electricity supplier does not provide an earth terminal to the consumer.

Because soil has a higher resistance than a metal wire, TT systems usually cannot rely on a standard fuse alone for protection. They typically require an RCD (Residual Current Device) to ensure the supply is disconnected quickly during a fault.
teachChecks.hint: In a TT system, the 'T' at the end means the consumer must provide their own connection to the ground.

teachChecks.title: TN-S and TN-C-S Systems
teachChecks.objective: Distinguish between TN-S and TN-C-S arrangements using the code letters and supply path.
retrievalTextLines: TN systems use the supplier's cable to provide the earth path back to the transformer. The T stands for Earth, and the N stands for Neutral.

In a TN-S system, the S stands for 'Separate.' This means the Neutral and Earth conductors are separate wires all the way from the transformer to the consumer. Usually, the lead or steel sheath of the supply cable acts as this separate earth path.

In a TN-C-S system, the C-S stands for 'Combined-Separate.' The Neutral and Earth are 'Combined' into a single conductor (called a PEN conductor) in the street, but they are 'Separated' at the consumer's intake (the cut-out).
TN-C-S is the most common modern system and is often referred to as PME, which stands for Protective Multiple Earthing.
teachChecks.hint: Look at the last letters: S means Separate wires, while C-S means they start Combined and then become Separate.

teachChecks.title: CPC and Earthing Conductor
teachChecks.objective: Explain the difference between a CPC and an Earthing Conductor in ADS.
retrievalTextLines: We use specific names for earth wires based on their location in the system. A Circuit Protective Conductor, or CPC, is the earth wire that protects an individual circuit.

The CPC runs alongside the live and neutral wires in every cable (like the bare wire in Twin and Earth) to ensure that if a fault occurs in a specific light or socket, the current has a path back to the MET.
The Earthing Conductor is different; it is the single, main cable that connects the Main Earthing Terminal (MET) to the means of earthing (the supplier's terminal or a local rod).
To remember the difference: the CPC is for the 'branch' (the circuit), while the Earthing Conductor is the 'trunk' that connects the whole building to the Earth source.
teachChecks.hint: The CPC is inside the cables going to your sockets; the Earthing Conductor is the main green-and-yellow wire at the service head.

        "In a TN-S system, the S stands for 'Separate.' This means the Neutral and Earth conductors are separate wires all the way from the transformer to the consumer. Usually, the lead or steel sheath of the supply cable acts as this separate earth path.",
        "In a TN-C-S system, the C-S stands for 'Combined-Separate.' The Neutral and Earth are 'Combined' into a single conductor (called a PEN conductor) in the street, but they are 'Separated' at the consumer's intake (the cut-out).",
        "TN-C-S is the most common modern system and is often referred to as PME, which stands for Protective Multiple Earthing."
      ],
      "hint": "Look at the last letters: S means Separate wires, while C-S means they start Combined and then become Separate."
    },
    {
      "title": "CPC and Earthing Conductor",
      "objective": "Explain the difference between a CPC and an Earthing Conductor in ADS.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "We use specific names for earth wires based on their location in the system. A Circuit Protective Conductor, or CPC, is the earth wire that protects an individual circuit.",
        "The CPC runs alongside the live and neutral wires in every cable (like the bare wire in Twin and Earth) to ensure that if a fault occurs in a specific light or socket, the current has a path back to the MET.",
        "The Earthing Conductor is different; it is the single, main cable that connects the Main Earthing Terminal (MET) to the means of earthing (the supplier's terminal or a local rod).",
        "To remember the difference: the CPC is for the 'branch' (the circuit), while the Earthing Conductor is the 'trunk' that connects the whole building to the Earth source."
      ],
      "hint": "The CPC is inside the cables going to your sockets; the Earthing Conductor is the main green-and-yellow wire at the service head."
    }
  ]
}

Critical rules:
- basicQuestions must contain exactly 3 items.
- Each basic question must test one taught fact only.
- Each basic question answerGuidance must contain 2-5 checkable phrases or ideas.
- Never ask the learner to supply multiple separate facts in one basic question.
- Prefer single-fact or single-term checks that support precise corrective feedback.
- If a question asks for the specific term, name, abbreviation expansion, code letter, or technical label, the first answerGuidance item must be the exact target from the lesson vocabulary.
- If a question defines something and asks for its name or type, answerGuidance must contain the exact technical term that matches that definition.
- For exact-term questions, answerGuidance may include only tight equivalents of that same target. Do not accept loose descriptive paraphrases, topic labels, or nearby concepts from other questions.
- If a question asks what one code letter stands for, answerGuidance must contain only that code-letter expansion word or a tight grammatical variant, not a component name or neighbouring concept.
- Keep each answerGuidance list local to its own question only. Do not copy valid answers from adjacent questions in the same step.
- The deeper question must connect the explanation facts and ask why/how/what-must-be-true.
- deeperAnswerGuidance must contain 3-6 checkable ideas.

teachChecks.title: Explanation A: The Role of ADS

basicQuestions.questionText: What does the abbreviation ADS stand for?
What is the primary safety purpose of the ADS measure?
Besides the earthing system, what other component is required to disconnect the power during a fault?

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: How does the earthing system enable a protective device to operate during a fault?
deeperAnswerGuidance: Provides a low-resistance path
Allows high fault current to flow
Triggers the fuse or circuit breaker
Disconnects the supply quickly
teachChecks.hint: Think about how a fuse knows when to blow; it needs a path for the fault current to travel through.

teachChecks.title: Explanation B: The Main Earthing Terminal and Bonding

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the name of the central hub where all protective conductors meet?
Which conductor is used to connect extraneous-conductive-parts to the MET?
What is a technical example of an extraneous-conductive-part?

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: Why must metallic water pipes be connected to the MET even though they are not part of the electrical system?
deeperAnswerGuidance: Ensures all metal parts are at the same voltage
Maintains the same potential
Prevents a dangerous voltage difference
Stops shock between pipes and appliances
teachChecks.hint: The MET is the 'meeting point' for all safety wires in the building.

teachChecks.title: TT Earthing Systems

basicQuestions.questionText: In the code TT, what does the second letter T stand for?
What physical component must a consumer install to connect a TT system to the ground?
Which specific protective device is typically required in a TT system to ensure fast disconnection?

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: Why is a standard fuse often unable to provide ADS in a TT system without additional devices?
deeperAnswerGuidance: Soil has a higher resistance than metal
Fault current is too low to blow a fuse
Resistance of the earth electrode path
RCD is needed for higher sensitivity
teachChecks.hint: In a TT system, the 'T' at the end means the consumer must provide their own connection to the ground.

teachChecks.title: TN-S and TN-C-S Systems

basicQuestions.questionText: What does the letter S stand for in a TN-S system?
At what specific point in a TN-C-S system are the neutral and earth conductors separated?
What is the common industry name for a TN-C-S earthing arrangement?

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: How does the supply path of a TN-S system differ from a TN-C-S system before it reaches the property?
deeperAnswerGuidance: TN-S uses a separate earth path like a cable sheath
TN-C-S uses a combined neutral and earth conductor
TN-C-S uses a PEN conductor in the street
TN-S has separate wires all the way from the transformer
teachChecks.hint: Look at the last letters: S means Separate wires, while C-S means they start Combined and then become Separate.

teachChecks.title: CPC and Earthing Conductor

basicQuestions.questionText: What does the abbreviation CPC stand for?
Which conductor connects the Main Earthing Terminal to the means of earthing?
Where is a CPC physically located within an installation?

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: Why is the CPC considered the 'branch' while the Earthing Conductor is considered the 'trunk' of the earthing system?
deeperAnswerGuidance: CPC protects a single specific circuit
Earthing Conductor connects the whole building to the source
Fault current travels from the CPC to the MET
The Earthing Conductor is the final link to the earth source
teachChecks.hint: The CPC is inside the cables going to your sockets; the Earthing Conductor is the main green-and-yellow wire at the service head.

Unit: 203 - Electrical installations technology

Learning outcome: LO4 - Requirements for earthing systems

Lesson: 203-4A - Earthing Systems and ADS Components

Topic focus: Earthing Systems and ADS Components

Prerequisites: 203-3C

Must-have topics: earthing system types; ADS component parts

Assessment criteria in scope:
- 203.LO4.AC4.1: Identify earthing system types
- 203.LO4.AC4.2: Identify ADS component parts

Lesson split rationale: Introduces the main types of earthing arrangements and the components required for Automatic Disconnection of Supply.

In-scope teaching anchors: earthing system types; ADS component parts

Out-of-scope topics: Exposed and Extraneous Conductive Parts; Earth Loop Impedance Path

- Wrong belief: Any related term means the same thing in every context. | Correct belief: Terms in this lesson are distinct and must be used precisely.

      "simpleDefinition": "Automatic Disconnection of Supply; a safety measure that cuts power quickly during a fault to prevent electric shock.",

      "term": "TN-C-S System",
      "simpleDefinition": "An earthing arrangement where neutral and earth are combined in the supply cable but separated at the consumer's intake.",

      "misconception": "Thinking the earth and neutral remain combined throughout the entire internal installation."

{
  "teachChecks": [
    {
      "title": "Explanation A: The Role of ADS",
      "objective": "Explanation A",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "ADS stands for Automatic Disconnection of Supply. It is the primary safety measure used to prevent electric shock in an installation.",
        "When a fault occurs—such as a live wire touching a metal casing—the ADS system must detect the fault and 'auto-kill' the power almost instantly.",
        "ADS is not just a single device; it is a combination of the earthing system and a protective device, such as a fuse or circuit breaker.",
        "The earthing system provides a low-resistance path for the fault current to flow. This high current then causes the protective device to trip or blow, disconnecting the supply before a person can be harmed by touching the live metalwork."
      ],
      "hint": "Think about how a fuse knows when to blow; it needs a path for the fault current to travel through."
    },
    {
      "title": "Explanation B: The Main Earthing Terminal and Bonding",
      "objective": "Explanation B",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "Every installation has a central safety hub called the Main Earthing Terminal, or MET. The MET is the master junction point where all protective conductors meet.",
        "One of the key connections to the MET is the Main Protective Bonding Conductor. This conductor is used to connect 'extraneous-conductive-parts' to the earthing system.",
        "Extraneous-conductive-parts are metal items that are not part of the electrical system but could introduce a voltage from outside, such as metallic gas, oil, or water pipes.",
        "By linking these pipes to the MET, we ensure that all accessible metal parts stay at the same voltage (potential). This prevents a dangerous voltage difference that could cause a shock if you touched a pipe and a faulty appliance at the same time."
      ],
      "hint": "The MET is the 'meeting point' for all safety wires in the building."
    },
    {
      "title": "TT Earthing Systems",
      "objective": "Identify TT systems and explain the role of the local earth electrode.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "In a TT system, the letters stand for 'Terre-Terre' (French for Earth-Earth).",
        "The first T indicates that the source (the supplier's transformer) is connected to Earth. The second T indicates that the consumer's installation is connected to Earth via a local earth electrode.",
        "An earth electrode is a physical metal rod, plate, or mesh driven into the soil at the property. In a TT arrangement, the electricity supplier does not provide an earth terminal to the consumer.",
        "Because soil has a higher resistance than a metal wire, TT systems usually cannot rely on a standard fuse alone for protection. They typically require an RCD (Residual Current Device) to ensure the supply is disconnected quickly during a fault."
      ],
      "hint": "In a TT system, the 'T' at the end means the consumer must provide their own connection to the ground."
    },
    {
      "title": "TN-S and TN-C-S Systems",
      "objective": "Distinguish between TN-S and TN-C-S arrangements using the code letters and supply path.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "TN systems use the supplier's cable to provide the earth path back to the transformer. The T stands for Earth, and the N stands for Neutral.",
        "In a TN-S system, the S stands for 'Separate.' This means the Neutral and Earth conductors are separate wires all the way from the transformer to the consumer. Usually, the lead or steel sheath of the supply cable acts as this separate earth path.",
        "In a TN-C-S system, the C-S stands for 'Combined-Separate.' The Neutral and Earth are 'Combined' into a single conductor (called a PEN conductor) in the street, but they are 'Separated' at the consumer's intake (the cut-out).",
        "TN-C-S is the most common modern system and is often referred to as PME, which stands for Protective Multiple Earthing."
      ],
      "hint": "Look at the last letters: S means Separate wires, while C-S means they start Combined and then become Separate."
    },
    {
      "title": "CPC and Earthing Conductor",
      "objective": "Explain the difference between a CPC and an Earthing Conductor in ADS.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "We use specific names for earth wires based on their location in the system. A Circuit Protective Conductor, or CPC, is the earth wire that protects an individual circuit.",
        "The CPC runs alongside the live and neutral wires in every cable (like the bare wire in Twin and Earth) to ensure that if a fault occurs in a specific light or socket, the current has a path back to the MET.",
        "The Earthing Conductor is different; it is the single, main cable that connects the Main Earthing Terminal (MET) to the means of earthing (the supplier's terminal or a local rod).",
        "To remember the difference: the CPC is for the 'branch' (the circuit), while the Earthing Conductor is the 'trunk' that connects the whole building to the Earth source."
      ],
      "hint": "The CPC is inside the cables going to your sockets; the Earthing Conductor is the main green-and-yellow wire at the service head."

output.rawText:
{
  "title": "Worked Example: Identifying Earthing Components",
  "objective": "Correctly identify the earthing system type and the specific names of the conductors used in an ADS setup.",
  "retrievalTextLines": [
    "Scenario: You are inspecting a modern domestic property. At the service head (the cut-out), you see that the supplier has provided an earth terminal that is linked directly to the neutral. A 16mm² green-and-yellow cable runs from this terminal to a central copper busbar.",
    "Step 1: Identify the Earthing System. Because the neutral and earth are combined in the supplier's cable but separated at the intake point, this is a TN-C-S system (often called PME).",
    "Step 2: Identify the Main Earthing Conductor. The 16mm² cable connecting the supplier's terminal to the copper busbar (the Main Earthing Terminal or MET) is the Earthing Conductor. It is the primary link to the earth source.",
    "Step 3: Identify the Bonding. You see 10mm² cables running from the MET to the incoming gas and water services. These are the Main Protective Bonding Conductors, used to keep all metalwork at the same potential.",
    "Step 4: Identify the Circuit Protection. Looking inside the consumer unit, you see the green-and-yellow cores of the twin-and-earth cables connected to the earth bar. These are the Circuit Protective Conductors (CPCs).",
    "Final Result: The installation is a TN-C-S system. The Earthing Conductor provides the main connection, while CPCs provide the fault path for individual circuits.",
    "Takeaway: Precision matters. The 'Earthing Conductor' is the main link to the source, the 'CPC' is for the individual circuit, and 'Bonding' is for the pipes. They are not interchangeable terms."
  ],
  "hint": "Focus on where the wire starts and ends. If it goes to a pipe, it's bonding. If it goes to a socket, it's a CPC. If it goes to the supplier's terminal, it's the Earthing Conductor."
}

parsed.title: Worked Example: Identifying Earthing Components
parsed.objective: Correctly identify the earthing system type and the specific names of the conductors used in an ADS setup.
retrievalTextLines: Scenario: You are inspecting a modern domestic property. At the service head (the cut-out), you see that the supplier has provided an earth terminal that is linked directly to the neutral. A 16mm² green-and-yellow cable runs from this terminal to a central copper busbar.
Step 1: Identify the Earthing System. Because the neutral and earth are combined in the supplier's cable but separated at the intake point, this is a TN-C-S system (often called PME).

Step 2: Identify the Main Earthing Conductor. The 16mm² cable connecting the supplier's terminal to the copper busbar (the Main Earthing Terminal or MET) is the Earthing Conductor. It is the primary link to the earth source.

Step 3: Identify the Bonding. You see 10mm² cables running from the MET to the incoming gas and water services. These are the Main Protective Bonding Conductors, used to keep all metalwork at the same potential.

Step 4: Identify the Circuit Protection. Looking inside the consumer unit, you see the green-and-yellow cores of the twin-and-earth cables connected to the earth bar. These are the Circuit Protective Conductors (CPCs).
Final Result: The installation is a TN-C-S system. The Earthing Conductor provides the main connection, while CPCs provide the fault path for individual circuits.
Takeaway: Precision matters. The 'Earthing Conductor' is the main link to the source, the 'CPC' is for the individual circuit, and 'Bonding' is for the pipes. They are not interchangeable terms.
parsed.hint: Focus on where the wire starts and ends. If it goes to a pipe, it's bonding. If it goes to a socket, it's a CPC. If it goes to the supplier's terminal, it's the Earthing Conductor.

Unit: 203 - Electrical installations technology

Learning outcome: LO4 - Requirements for earthing systems

Lesson: 203-4A - Earthing Systems and ADS Components

Topic focus: Earthing Systems and ADS Components

Prerequisites: 203-3C

Must-have topics: earthing system types; ADS component parts

Assessment criteria in scope:
- 203.LO4.AC4.1: Identify earthing system types
- 203.LO4.AC4.2: Identify ADS component parts

Lesson split rationale: Introduces the main types of earthing arrangements and the components required for Automatic Disconnection of Supply.

In-scope teaching anchors: earthing system types; ADS component parts

Out-of-scope topics: Exposed and Extraneous Conductive Parts; Earth Loop Impedance Path

Known misconceptions to handle:
- Wrong belief: Any related term means the same thing in every context. | Correct belief: Terms in this lesson are distinct and must be used precisely.
- Wrong belief: You can answer checks without using taught explanation content. | Correct belief: Checks must be answered from concepts taught in the explanation blocks.

Vocabulary output:
{
  "terms": [
    {
      "term": "ADS",
      "simpleDefinition": "Automatic Disconnection of Supply; a safety measure that cuts power quickly during a fault to prevent electric shock.",
      "anchor": "The system's 'auto-kill' switch for faults.",
      "misconception": "Thinking ADS is just a single device like a fuse; it is actually the combination of earthing and protective devices working together."
    },
    {
      "term": "TT System",
      "simpleDefinition": "An earthing arrangement where the consumer provides their own connection to Earth using a local electrode.",
      "anchor": "The 'Earth-to-Earth' system using a rod in the ground.",
      "misconception": "Believing the electricity supplier provides the earth connection in a TT system."
    },
    {
      "term": "TN-S System",
      "simpleDefinition": "An earthing arrangement where the supplier provides a separate earth conductor (usually the cable sheath) back to the transformer.",
      "anchor": "The 'Separate' system with a dedicated earth path from the source.",
      "misconception": "Confusing the cable sheath earth with a combined neutral-earth conductor."
    },
    {
      "term": "TN-C-S System",
      "simpleDefinition": "An earthing arrangement where neutral and earth are combined in the supply cable but separated at the consumer's intake.",
      "anchor": "The 'Combined' supply system, often called PME.",
      "misconception": "Thinking the earth and neutral remain combined throughout the entire internal installation."
    },
    {
      "term": "CPC",
      "simpleDefinition": "Circuit Protective Conductor; the specific earth wire that protects an individual circuit.",
      "anchor": "The circuit's personal safety wire.",
      "misconception": "Using the term CPC to describe the main earthing conductor that connects to the supplier's terminal."
    },
    {
      "term": "Earthing Conductor",
      "simpleDefinition": "The main conductor that connects the installation's Main Earthing Terminal (MET) to the means of earthing.",
      "anchor": "The master link between the house and the Earth source.",
      "misconception": "Confusing this with bonding conductors which connect to gas or water pipes."
    },
    {
      "term": "Earth Electrode",
      "simpleDefinition": "A metal rod, plate, or mesh driven into the ground to provide a physical connection to Earth.",
      "anchor": "The physical spike in the soil.",
      "misconception": "Thinking every house needs an electrode; they are primarily required for TT systems."
    },
    {
      "term": "Main Protective Bonding Conductor",
      "simpleDefinition": "A conductor used to connect extraneous-conductive-parts (like metal water pipes) to the Main Earthing Terminal.",
      "anchor": "The safety link for incoming metal services.",
      "misconception": "Thinking bonding conductors are meant to carry fault current from a circuit like a CPC does."
    }
  ],
  "anchorPhrases": [
    "TT uses a local earth electrode.",
    "TN-S has a separate earth path back to the source.",
    "TN-C-S combines neutral and earth in the supply.",
    "The CPC protects the individual circuit.",
    "The Earthing Conductor connects the MET to the earth source."
  ],
  "misconceptionTargets": [
    "Terms in this lesson are distinct and must be used precisely.",
    "The CPC and Earthing Conductor have different roles in the ADS path.",
    "The supplier only provides the earth terminal in TN systems, not TT."
  ]
}

Critical rules:
- guidedPractice retrieval and task must clearly mirror the worked example pattern.
- practice must be more independent than guidedPractice.
- Do not use these phases to smuggle in extra untaught chunk content that should have been its own teach/check section.
- Each phase must contain one coherent task with one response target type.
- Do not bundle several unrelated sub-questions, several scenarios, or mixed outputs into a single response field.
- Do not write identify-plus-explain bundles in one response box. If synthesis is needed, rewrite as one coherent explanation task.
- Do not ask for long ordered lists, “identify all of these”, or “answer 1, 2, 3” style tasks in these phases.
- If the learner must compare two items, the question must still produce one coherent explanation, not two separate hidden marking jobs.
- If a task currently asks for one conversion plus one classification, rewrite it so the learner gives one combined markable answer string or reduce it to one single target.
- answerGuidance must map directly to the exact response expected from that one task.
- If a task asks for a specific technical term or named item, answerGuidance must prioritise the exact target only.
- answerGuidance arrays must be concrete and gradeable.
- Never return empty questionText, empty retrievalTextLines, or empty answerGuidance for guidedPractice or practice.
- If you are unsure, generate a narrower single-target task instead of leaving any field blank.
output.rawText:
{
  "guidedPractice": {
    "title": "Guided Practice",
    "objective": "Identify a TN-S earthing system and its primary conductor components.",
    "retrievalTextLines": [
      "Step 1: Identify the System. If the supplier provides an earth via a separate path (like the metal sheath of the cable) that is not connected to the neutral at the intake, it is a TN-S system.",
      "Step 2: Identify the Main Link. The conductor that connects the supplier's earth terminal (on the cable sheath) to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET) is the Earthing Conductor.",
      "Step 3: Identify the Circuit Protection. The green-and-yellow wire within a final circuit (like a lighting circuit) is the Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC)."
    ],
    "questionText": "You are working on an older property where the earth connection is a clamp fixed to the lead sheath of the incoming supply cable. A 16mm² cable runs from this clamp to the MET. Identify the earthing system type and the specific technical name of that 16mm² cable.",
    "answerGuidance": [
      "TN-S and Earthing Conductor",
      "TN-S; Earthing Conductor",
      "TN-S system and Earthing Conductor"
    ],
    "hint": "Look at the 'S' in TN-S—it stands for 'Separate'. The cable connecting the source to the MET is always the Earthing Conductor."
  },
  "practice": {
    "title": "Practice",
    "objective": "Identify a TT earthing system and the role of the local earth electrode.",
    "retrievalTextLines": [
      "In a TT system, the supplier does not provide an earth terminal.",
      "The consumer must provide their own connection to the mass of earth.",
      "This is achieved using a physical component driven into the ground, which is then connected to the MET via the Earthing Conductor."
    ],
    "questionText": "A remote farm building has no earth provided by the DNO (supplier). The electrician has driven a copper-clad steel rod into the ground to provide the earth path. State the earthing system type and the technical name for the copper-clad rod.",
    "answerGuidance": [
      "TT and Earth Electrode",
      "TT; Earth Electrode",
      "TT system and Earth Electrode"
    ],
    "hint": "If the consumer provides the earth themselves using a rod in the soil, it is a TT system. Think about the specific name for that rod in the ADS range."
  }
}

Step 2: Identify the Main Link. The conductor that connects the supplier's earth terminal (on the cable sheath) to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET) is the Earthing Conductor.
Step 3: Identify the Circuit Protection. The green-and-yellow wire within a final circuit (like a lighting circuit) is the Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC).

practice.title: Practice
practice.objective: Identify a TT earthing system and the role of the local earth electrode.
retrievalTextLines: In a TT system, the supplier does not provide an earth terminal.
The consumer must provide their own connection to the mass of earth.
This is achieved using a physical component driven into the ground, which is then connected to the MET via the Earthing Conductor.
practice.questionText: A remote farm building has no earth provided by the DNO (supplier). The electrician has driven a copper-clad steel rod into the ground to provide the earth path. State the earthing system type and the technical name for the copper-clad rod.
practice.hint: If the consumer provides the earth themselves using a rod in the soil, it is a TT system. Think about the specific name for that rod in the ADS range.

retrievalTextLines: Before we dive into earthing systems, think back to Unit 203-3C.
We looked at how circuits are protected from overcurrent and short circuits.

parsed.questionText: What is the primary mechanical or electrical action a protective device must perform the moment a fault is detected to ensure safety?

It must 'trip' or 'blow' to stop the flow of current immediately.
This action prevents damage to the installation and reduces the risk of electric shock.

lesson.title: Earthing Systems and ADS Components
lesson.subject: C&G 2365
lesson.tonePrompt: Teach like a strong electrical tutor: practical, direct, technically precise, concise, and natural.

steps.title: Intro
steps.objective: Orient the learner to Earthing Systems and ADS Components.
anchorFacts: This lesson focuses on Earthing Systems and ADS Components.
taskConstraints: Introduce the lesson naturally, but do not start the first teaching chunk yet.

steps.title: Explanation A: The Role of ADS
steps.objective: Explanation A
keyIdeas: ADS stands for Automatic Disconnection of Supply. It is the primary safety measure used to prevent electric shock in an installation.
When a fault occurs—such as a live wire touching a metal casing—the ADS system must detect the fault and 'auto-kill' the power almost instantly.
ADS is not just a single device; it is a combination of the earthing system and a protective device, such as a fuse or circuit breaker.

The earthing system provides a low-resistance path for the fault current to flow. This high current then causes the protective device to trip or blow, disconnecting the supply before a person can be harmed by touching the live metalwork.
Explanation A
anchorFacts: ADS stands for Automatic Disconnection of Supply. It is the primary safety measure used to prevent electric shock in an installation.
When a fault occurs—such as a live wire touching a metal casing—the ADS system must detect the fault and 'auto-kill' the power almost instantly.
ADS is not just a single device; it is a combination of the earthing system and a protective device, such as a fuse or circuit breaker.

The earthing system provides a low-resistance path for the fault current to flow. This high current then causes the protective device to trip or blow, disconnecting the supply before a person can be harmed by touching the live metalwork.
ADS is the correct expansion for this abbreviation.
misconceptionsToWatch: Think about how a fuse knows when to blow; it needs a path for the fault current to travel through.
steps.questionIntent: Explanation A

basicQuestions.questionText: What does the abbreviation ADS stand for?
What is the primary safety purpose of the ADS measure?
Besides the earthing system, what other component is required to disconnect the power during a fault?

steps.deeperQuestionText: What is the main way the earthing system enable a protective device to operate during a fault?
steps.deeperQuestionIntent: Explanation A
deeperAnswerGuidance: Provides a low-resistance path, Allows high fault current to flow, Triggers the fuse or circuit breaker, and Disconnects the supply quickly
steps.hint: Think about how a fuse knows when to blow; it needs a path for the fault current to travel through.

steps.title: Explanation B: The Main Earthing Terminal and Bonding
steps.objective: Explanation B
keyIdeas: Every installation has a central safety hub called the Main Earthing Terminal, or MET. The MET is the master junction point where all protective conductors meet.
One of the key connections to the MET is the Main Protective Bonding Conductor. This conductor is used to connect 'extraneous-conductive-parts' to the earthing system.
Extraneous-conductive-parts are metal items that are not part of the electrical system but could introduce a voltage from outside, such as metallic gas, oil, or water pipes.

By linking these pipes to the MET, we ensure that all accessible metal parts stay at the same voltage (potential). This prevents a dangerous voltage difference that could cause a shock if you touched a pipe and a faulty appliance at the same time.
Explanation B
anchorFacts: Every installation has a central safety hub called the Main Earthing Terminal, or MET. The MET is the master junction point where all protective conductors meet.
One of the key connections to the MET is the Main Protective Bonding Conductor. This conductor is used to connect 'extraneous-conductive-parts' to the earthing system.
Extraneous-conductive-parts are metal items that are not part of the electrical system but could introduce a voltage from outside, such as metallic gas, oil, or water pipes.

By linking these pipes to the MET, we ensure that all accessible metal parts stay at the same voltage (potential). This prevents a dangerous voltage difference that could cause a shock if you touched a pipe and a faulty appliance at the same time.
Main Protective Bonding Conductor is the name of the central hub where all protective conductors meet.
misconceptionsToWatch: The MET is the 'meeting point' for all safety wires in the building.
steps.questionIntent: Explanation B

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the name of the central hub where all protective conductors meet?

steps.deeperQuestionText: What is the main reason metallic water pipes be connected to the MET even though they are not part of the electrical system?
steps.deeperQuestionIntent: Explanation B
deeperAnswerGuidance: Ensures all metal parts are at the same voltage, Maintains the same potential, Prevents a dangerous voltage difference, and Stops shock between pipes and appliances
steps.hint: The MET is the 'meeting point' for all safety wires in the building.

steps.title: TT Earthing Systems
steps.objective: Identify TT systems and explain the role of the local earth electrode.
keyIdeas: In a TT system, the letters stand for 'Terre-Terre' (French for Earth-Earth).
The first T indicates that the source (the supplier's transformer) is connected to Earth. The second T indicates that the consumer's installation is connected to Earth via a local earth electrode.
An earth electrode is a physical metal rod, plate, or mesh driven into the soil at the property. In a TT arrangement, the electricity supplier does not provide an earth terminal to the consumer.

Because soil has a higher resistance than a metal wire, TT systems usually cannot rely on a standard fuse alone for protection. They typically require an RCD (Residual Current Device) to ensure the supply is disconnected quickly during a fault.
Identify TT systems and explain the role of the local earth electrode.
